source.title: Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3Val125Ile and Pho3Asn134Asp revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The initial problem was that the high-tyrosol-producing S. cerevisiae strain S26, constructed via extensive metabolic engineering, suffered from impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify the genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms behind the improved phenotype, and validate key mutations via reverse engineering.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: ALE of S. cerevisiae S26 yielded three evolved strains. S26-AE2 showed 15.33% higher biomass (17.82 g DCW/L) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol in 100 g/L glucose. Transcriptome analysis revealed reduced HXK2 expression (less glucose repression), increased pyruvate synthesis genes, and higher TCA cycle gene expression in 20 g/L glucose. SNP analysis identified SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations. Reverse engineering confirmed SNZ3 mutation increased tyrosol by 17.01% in 100 g/L glucose.
process_signals: ALE successfully restored growth and increased tyrosol production.
Transcriptome and SNP analyses provided mechanistic hypotheses.
Reverse engineering validated the role of SNZ3 mutation.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Tyrosol is a valuable drug precursor. S. cerevisiae S26 was previously engineered for high tyrosol yield but excessive modification reduced robustness and growth, limiting industrial use. ALE is a proven method to improve growth defects and understand molecular evolution. This study aimed to use ALE to restore growth and increase tyrosol, then apply transcriptomics, SNP analysis, and reverse engineering to elucidate mechanisms.
process_signals: Initial strain S26 had growth impairment due to metabolic engineering.
ALE was chosen to restore growth and potentially improve tyrosol production.
Plan to combine ALE with omics and reverse engineering for mechanistic insight.
failures_or_limits: Excessive metabolic modification decreased yeast robustness.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26 for improved growth phenotypes
section_gists.gist: ALE was performed in YEPD with stepwise glucose increases: Stage 1 (20→40 g/L, 16 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE1; Stage 2 (40→80 g/L, 11.75 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE2; Stage 3 (80→125 g/L, 11.95 h avg passage) yielded S26-AE3. SEM showed some cell damage in S26-AE2 but not worsened in S26-AE3. Spot assays showed all evolved strains grew better than S26 on 20 and 100 g/L glucose.
process_signals: ALE successfully improved growth on high glucose.
Passage time decreased from stage 1 to 2, indicating adaptation.
S26-AE2 showed cell damage but still better growth.
methods: Adaptive laboratory evolution in EVOL cells with automatic passaging at OD600=15
failures_or_limits: S26-AE2 showed obvious cell damage under SEM, possibly due to premature stationary phase or high sugar.

section_gists.heading: Changes in the fermentation performance of evolutionary strains
section_gists.gist: In 100 g/L glucose YEPD, S26 entered stationary phase at 48 h (OD600 36.39, 15.45 g DCW/L). S26-AE1 biomass was 8.99% higher. S26-AE2 entered stationary phase earlier (24 h), OD600 38.45, biomass 17.82 g DCW/L (15.33% higher). S26-AE3 also early stationary phase, OD600 39.14. Tyrosol: S26 max 483.35 mg/L at 60 h; S26-AE1 not significantly different; S26-AE2 reached 817.83 mg/L at 48 h (69.20% higher than S26-AE1); S26-AE3 553.79 mg/L at 72 h. Glucose consumption: S26 residual 89.30 g/L at 12 h; S26-AE1 38.05 g/L; S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose by 12 h. Ethanol: S26 4.20 g/L, S26-AE1 23.08 g/L, S26-AE2 34.16 g/L, S26-AE3 36.12 g/L at 12 h. Trehalose: S26 and S26-AE1 accumulated extracellular trehalose; S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 produced less.
process_signals: S26-AE2 showed best combination of growth and tyrosol production.
Evolved strains consumed glucose faster and produced more ethanol, indicating metabolic shift.
S26-AE2 selected for further analysis due to superior performance.
methods: Fermentation in YEPD with 100 g/L glucose
OD600 and dry cell weight measurement
failures_or_limits: S26-AE3 had lower tyrosol than S26-AE2 despite good growth.
S26-AE1 did not improve tyrosol significantly.

section_gists.heading: Transcriptome analysis of the mechanisms underlying adaptive evolution leading to increased tyrosol production in strain S26-AE2
section_gists.gist: Transcriptome compared S26 and S26-AE2 in 20 and 100 g/L glucose. In 100 vs 20 g/L, S26 had 807 up, 583 down; S26-AE2 had 779 up, 621 down. S26-AE2 vs S26 in 20 g/L: 36 up, 1 down; in 100 g/L: 9 up, 16 down. Key findings: HXT5 (low/medium-affinity glucose transporter) upregulated in S26-AE2 in 20 g/L. HXK2 expression lower in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L, suggesting reduced glucose repression. Pyruvate synthesis genes (PYK2, PDC1, PDC5, PDC6) upregulated in S26-AE2. TCA cycle genes (CIT1, ACO1, IDH1, IDH2, KGD1, KGD2, LSC1, LSC2, SDH1, SDH2, SDH3, SDH4, FUM1, MDH1) mostly upregulated in S26-AE2 in 20 g/L. Tyrosol synthesis genes (ARO10, ARO8, ARO9) upregulated in S26-AE2.
process_signals: Transcriptome provided hypotheses for improved growth and tyrosol: reduced glucose repression, increased pyruvate and TCA cycle, upregulated tyrosol pathway.
Differences in gene expression between strains and conditions guided focus on specific pathways.
failures_or_limits: Transcriptome is correlative; causal links need validation.

section_gists.heading: SNP mutation analysis of the adaptive laboratory evolutionary strain S26-AE2
section_gists.gist: Genome sequencing of S26-AE2 identified 19 SNP mutations across comparisons. Five genes had SNPs: MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, PHO3. SNZ3 is a pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase subunit, induced in stationary phase and thiamine absence. PHO3 is a constitutively expressed acid phosphatase. These mutations were hypothesized to affect thiamine/pyridoxine metabolism and thus tyrosol production.
process_signals: SNP analysis narrowed down candidate mutations responsible for evolved phenotype.
SNZ3 and PHO3 selected for further study based on known functions.
failures_or_limits: Only 19 SNPs found; other mutations may exist outside coding regions.

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking analysis of the SNZ3 and PHO3 mutants binding to small molecules
section_gists.gist: Docking simulations: PHO3 Asn134Asp mutation decreased Vina score for thiamine phosphate to -6.6 (improved affinity) and for thiamine to -5.4. Residue interactions changed. SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation decreased Vina score for D-ribose 5-phosphate to -5.7, but no residue change; for pyridoxal, score unchanged (-5.6) but THR4 and ALA69 binding increased. Results suggest mutations may enhance substrate binding.
process_signals: Molecular docking provided computational support for functional impact of mutations.
Improved binding affinity suggests mutations could increase enzyme activity.
failures_or_limits: Docking is in silico prediction; experimental validation needed.
SNZ3 mutation did not change binding residues, only score.

section_gists.heading: Reverse engineering verification improves yeast growth and robustness
section_gists.gist: CRISPR-Cas9 used to introduce SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations into S26, creating single and double mutants. In 20 g/L glucose, S26-SNZ3 Mut increased tyrosol by 5.61% at 60 h. In 100 g/L glucose, S26-SNZ3 Mut increased tyrosol by 17.01% at 72 h. Double mutant increased tyrosol by 11.37% at 72 h. PHO3 single mutant effect not explicitly stated. Results confirm SNZ3 mutation promotes tyrosol production, especially in high glucose.
process_signals: Reverse engineering validated SNZ3 mutation's positive effect on tyrosol.
Double mutant did not outperform single SNZ3 mutant, suggesting possible interaction or saturation.
methods: Fermentation in YEPD with 20 or 100 g/L glucose
failures_or_limits: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported.
Double mutant had lower tyrosol increase than SNZ3 single mutant (11.37% vs 17.01%).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: ALE restored growth and improved tyrosol in S26-AE2. Transcriptome revealed changes in glycolysis, TCA cycle, and tyrosol synthesis. Genome analysis identified PHO3 and SNZ3 mutations. Molecular docking suggested enhanced substrate binding. Reverse engineering confirmed SNZ3 mutation increases tyrosol. Hypothesis: mutations increase pyridoxine and thiamine synthesis, boosting phosphoketolase activity and E4P precursor for tyrosol. Not all mutations beneficial; PHO6 mutant auxotrophic for thiamine.
process_signals: Integrated omics and reverse engineering to propose mechanism.
Acknowledged that not all mutations are beneficial.
Proposed thiamine/pyridoxine link to tyrosol via phosphoketolase.
failures_or_limits: Mechanism is hypothetical; direct enzyme activity not measured.
Other mutations (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: ALE restored growth of S26. Transcriptome showed expression differences in glycolysis, TCA cycle, tyrosol synthesis. Molecular docking and reverse engineering validated SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increases tyrosol. Study provides basis for metabolic engineering of tyrosol production.
process_signals: Successfully achieved goal of restoring growth and improving tyrosol.
Validated key mutation.

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution
section_gists.gist: S26 was evolved in EVOL cells with automatic passaging when OD600 reached 15. Initial glucose 20 g/L, gradually increased. Samples collected at each stage, preserved in glycerol. Average passage time calculated per stage.
process_signals: ALE protocol: automatic passaging at fixed OD600, stepwise glucose increase.
Selection based on growth under increasing glucose stress.
methods: EVOL cell system for automatic passaging

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: OD600 measured by spectrophotometer. Tyrosol and trehalose by HPLC with C18 column, gradient elution, UV detection at 224 nm for tyrosol, refractive index for trehalose. Glucose and ethanol by biosensor. Glycerol by TG assay kit. Cell morphology by SEM.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: ALE under increasing glucose selected for mutations that restore growth and enhance tyrosol production by altering central carbon metabolism and thiamine/pyridoxine synthesis.
SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increases pyridoxal phosphate synthesis, which may boost thiamine production, activating phosphoketolase and increasing E4P precursor for tyrosol.
PHO3 Asn134Asp mutation may improve thiamine phosphate hydrolysis, contributing to thiamine availability, but its effect on tyrosol is less clear than SNZ3.
Transcriptomic changes (reduced HXK2, increased TCA cycle) support a shift toward respiratory metabolism and reduced glucose repression, improving growth efficiency.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact effect of PHO3 Asn134Asp single mutation on growth and tyrosol? The text does not clearly state its individual impact.
Why did the double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) show lower tyrosol increase than SNZ3 single mutant? Possible epistasis or metabolic burden.
Are the transcriptomic changes directly caused by the identified SNPs, or are they secondary adaptations?
What are the roles of other SNP-containing genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3)?
Is the proposed thiamine-pyridoxine-phosphoketolase mechanism supported by direct enzyme activity or metabolite measurements?
Does the improved growth in high glucose come at a cost in other conditions?

events.summary: Excessive metabolic modification in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but decreased growth rate.
Adaptive laboratory evolution can restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S. cerevisiae S26.
Adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26 yielded three evolved strains.
Strain S26-AE2 achieved 15.33% higher biomass and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol in 100 g/L glucose.
Transcriptome analysis revealed reduced HXK2 expression in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose, suggesting reduced glucose repression.
Transcriptome analysis showed increased pyruvate synthesis genes and TCA cycle genes in S26-AE2.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk is the abstract and contains key events summarizing the entire study: problem, hypothesis, experimental results, transcriptome analysis, and validation.

events.summary: Adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26 was performed in three stages with stepwise glucose increases, yielding strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, and S26-AE3.

Stage 2: glucose 40→80 g/L, avg passage 11.75 h
Stage 3: glucose 80→125 g/L, avg passage 11.95 h

Fermentation in 100 g/L glucose YEPD showed S26-AE2 had 15.33% higher biomass and 69.20% higher tyrosol than S26-AE1, reaching 817.83 mg/L at 48 h.
methods_or_tools: Fermentation in YEPD with 100 g/L glucose
numbers_or_metrics: S26-AE2 biomass: 17.82 g DCW/L (15.33% higher than S26)
S26-AE2 tyrosol: 817.83 mg/L at 48 h (69.20% higher than S26-AE1)

events.summary: Evolved strains S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose by 12 h and produced more ethanol, indicating faster glucose utilization.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ALE process, fermentation performance, transcriptome, SNP analysis, and molecular docking, providing key experimental and computational events.

events.summary: Hypothesis that decreased HXK2 expression in S26-AE2 reduced glucose repression, contributing to improved growth.
PGI1 expression increased slightly in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose, possibly promoting glucose utilization.
Evolved strains reduced trehalose and glycerol production, indicating adaptation to high glucose stress.
Transcriptome comparison showed S26-AE2 had 36 upregulated and 1 downregulated gene vs S26 in 20 g/L glucose, and 9 upregulated and 16 downregulated in 100 g/L glucose.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains transcriptome analysis results and interpretation, including specific gene expression changes and hypotheses about glucose repression and stress adaptation.

events.summary: Transcriptome analysis reveals that PGI1 expression increased slightly in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose, suggesting enhanced glucose utilization.
Transcriptome analysis shows increased expression of GPD1, GPD2, and GPP2 in both S26 and S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose, with GPP2 slightly lower in S26-AE2, consistent with glycerol measurements.
Transcriptome analysis indicates that genes involved in pyruvate production (PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, PYK1) are upregulated in S26-AE2 compared to S26, suggesting increased pyruvate synthesis.
Transcriptome analysis shows low expression of PYK2 in the presence of 100 g/L glucose, and its absence does not affect growth rate on glucose.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains transcriptome analysis results focusing on glycolysis and pyruvate metabolism genes, providing mechanistic insights into the evolved strain's improved performance. No new experimental events beyond analysis are described.

events.summary: Transcriptome analysis revealed that under 20 g/L glucose, most TCA cycle genes were upregulated in S26-AE2 compared to S26, indicating more active TCA cycle in the evolved strain.
Expression of tyrosol synthesis genes ARO3, ARO4, ARO2, ARO7, TYR1, and ARO10 increased under 100 g/L glucose, explaining higher tyrosol in S26-AE2.
SNP mutation analysis of S26-AE2 identified 19 SNPs across comparisons, revealing five genes with mutations: MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, PHO3.
SNZ3 is a pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase subunit induced in stationary phase and thiamine absence; PHO3 is a constitutively expressed acid phosphatase.
Under 20 g/L glucose, glyoxylic acid cycle genes PYC1, MDH2, MDH3, MLS1, IDP2, ACS1, ACS2, ALD6, PDC6 were significantly higher in S26-AE2 than S26.
Increased TCA cycle activity promotes pyruvate consumption, explaining growth recovery through glucose evolution.
Under 20 g/L glucose, ZWF1 and RKI1 expression increased in S26-AE2, potentially increasing NADPH supply.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed transcriptome analysis results and SNP mutation identification, directly supporting mechanistic hypotheses.

events.summary: SNP mutation analysis identified 19 SNPs in S26-AE2, including nonsynonymous mutations in SNZ3 (Val125Ile) and PHO3 (Asn134Asp).
Molecular docking simulated PHO3 and SNZ3 mutants binding to substrates, showing improved affinity for thiamine phosphate and D-ribose 5-phosphate.
SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increased tyrosol production by 17.01% in 100 g/L glucose, confirming its role in enhancing tyrosol yield.
Double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) increased tyrosol by 11.37%, less than SNZ3 single mutant, suggesting possible interaction or saturation.
Docking showed PHO3 Asn134Asp mutation altered residue interactions with thiamine phosphate, reducing binding of PRO51, PRO363, MET365, GLY366 and increasing ALA348, ILE398, CYS408.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers SNP analysis, molecular docking, and reverse engineering validation, directly supporting the paper's mechanistic investigation.

events.summary: Hypothesized that SNZ3 mutation promotes pyridoxine utilization and growth, thereby increasing tyrosol production.
Reverse engineering confirmed that a single-nucleotide mutation in SNZ3 increases tyrosol concentration.
Transcriptome analysis revealed metabolic changes in glycolysis, TCA cycle, and tyrosol synthesis pathways in S26-AE2.
Speculated that metabolic changes contribute to growth recovery and tyrosol production.
Genome analysis detected mutations in PHO3 and SNZ3 genes.
Molecular docking showed that mutations enhance protein-small molecule interactions.
Reverse engineering verified that PHO3 and SNZ3 mutations are beneficial for tyrosol production.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion and conclusions summarizing key findings: transcriptome and genome analyses, molecular docking, reverse engineering validation, and mechanistic hypothesis linking mutations to tyrosol production via thiamine and phosphoketolase.

events.summary: ALE protocol designed with automatic passaging at OD600=15 and stepwise glucose increase.
HPLC method for tyrosol and trehalose quantification.
CRISPR-Cas9 system used for reverse engineering mutations.
Statistical analysis using t-test and visualization with GraphPad Prism and R.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily contains methods descriptions (ALE protocol, RNA extraction, differential expression analysis, HPLC, spot assay, molecular docking, CRISPR-Cas9, statistics). It provides design and experimental details but no new results or process evolution beyond what was already summarized in earlier sections.
This chunk contains only references, funding, author details, and declarations. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: Although excessive metabolic modification increases the production of tyrosol, it also causes a decrease in the growth rate of yeast.
this study attempted to restore the growth of S. cerevisiae through adaptive evolution and further improve tyrosol production.
After the adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26, three evolutionary strains were obtained.
The biomass of strain S26-AE2 reached 17.82 g DCW/L in the presence of 100 g/L glucose, which was 15.33% higher than that of S26, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L.
The transcriptome analysis revealed that, upon exposure to 100 g/L glucose, the S26-AE2 strain may reduce the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression through decreased HXK2 expression.

The expression of genes related to pyruvate synthesis was increased in strain S26-AE2. Meanwhile, the expression levels of most tricarboxylic acid cycle-related genes in S26-AE2 were increased when cultured with 20 g/L glucose.
In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.

A small amount of cell damage was observed in S26-AE1. S26-AE2 showed obvious cell damage, which may be attributed to the premature transition of cells into the stable phase or an increase in the sugar concentration.
the biomass of strain S26-AE2 was 17.82 g DCW/L at 48 h, which was 15.33% higher than that of strain S26.
the tyrosol production of S26-AE2 improved significantly, especially at 48 h, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L, which was 69.20% higher than the highest yield of strain S26-AE1.
The evolved strains S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose after 12 h and produced 34.16 g/L and 36.12 g/L ethanol, respectively
we speculated that after adaptive evolution, the decreased expression of HXK2 in strain S26-AE2 reduced the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression in cells
The expression of the PGI1 gene in strain S26-AE2 increased slightly under 100 g/L glucose conditions
the strains reduced the production of trehalose and glycerol, suggesting that the evolved strains gradually adapted to grow on 100 g/L glucose medium
Compared with S26, S26-AE2 presented 36 upregulated genes and 1 downregulated gene in the presence of 20 g/L glucose
Compared with the original strain S26, the evolutionary strain S26-AE2 had 9 upregulated genes and 16 downregulated genes in the presence of 100 g/L glucose
The expression of the PGI1 gene in strain S26-AE2 increased slightly under 100 g/L glucose conditions (Fig. 6A).
In the presence of 100 g/L glucose, the expression of the GPD1, GPD2, and GPP2 genes increased in both S26 and S26-AE2 (Fig. 6A).
The expression of the GPP2 gene in S26-AE2 was slightly lower than that in S26, which was consistent with the results of the glycerol determination (Fig. 4K).
The gene expression levels of PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, and PYK1, which are involved in pyruvate production, in strain S26-AE2 were higher than those in strain S26 (Fig. 6A).
the expression of PYK2 was low in the presence of 100 g/L glucose.
the absence of PYK2 has no effect on the specific growth rate when glucose is used as a carbon source
most of the genes involved in the TCA cycle were upregulated under 20 g/L glucose conditions
gene expression in strain S26-AE2 under 20 g/L glucose conditions was clearly significantly higher than that in strain S26
The increased expression of ARO3 and ARO4 promotes the formation of DAHP and increases tyrosol production, which also explains the increase in the tyrosol content in strain S26-AE2
The results revealed 19 SNP mutations in S26-AE2-100vsS26-20, S26-AE2-100vsS26-AE2-20, and S26-100vsS26-20.
five genes with SNP mutations, namely, MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, and PHO3
SNZ3 is a subunit of pyridoxal 5'-phosphate synthase and a member of a stationary phase-induced gene family.
PHO3 is a constitutively expressed acid phosphatase
the expression levels of the PYC1, MDH2, MDH3, MLS1, IDP2, ACS1, ACS2, ALD6, and PDC6 genes in the evolutionary strain were significantly higher than those in the original strain

An increase in TCA cycle activity promotes the consumption of pyruvate, which is derived mainly from the decomposition of glucose by cells. The TCA cycle promotes cell growth and energy generation, which in a certain sense explains the recovery of strain growth through glucose evolution.
the expression levels of ZWF1 and RK11 increased in strain S26-AE2
the overexpression of ZWF1 can increase the supply of NADPH in yeast cells

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.
PHO3 was mutated at residues 132, 134, and 420, and only amino acid residues 420 exhibited a nonsynonymous mutation.
After SNZ3 mutation, the Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule D-ribose 5-phosphate decreased to -5.7
Under 100 g/L glucose culture, tyrosol production by the SNZ3 mutant increased by 17.01% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h
the tyrosol production of the SNZ3 and PHO3 comutants increased by 11.37% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h
the interaction of the PRO51, PRO363, MET365, and GLY366 residues with small molecules was reduced, and the binding of the ALA348, ILE398, and CYS408 residues was increased

we speculated that the mutation promoted the utilisation and transport of pyridoxine. The increased utilisation of pyridoxine by yeast promoted the growth of yeast in 100 g/L glucose medium. The recovery of yeast growth further promoted the production of tyrosol.
Reverse engineering demonstrated that a single-nucleotide mutation in SNZ3 could increase the concentration of tyrosol.
the key metabolic changes were those in the glycolysis pathway, tricarboxylic acid cycle, and tyrosol synthesis pathway
We speculate that these metabolic changes may contribute to the recovery of yeast growth and the production of tyrosol.
we detected mutations in the key genes PHO3 and SNZ3
Our molecular docking results revealed that mutations in these genes enhance the interaction between proteins and small molecules.
we determined yeast growth and found that the mutation of the PHO3 and SNZ3 genes was indeed beneficial for the production of the growth substrate tyrosol in yeast
The initial glucose concentration of the evolution medium was 20 g/L, and the amount of added glucose gradually increased during the process of evolution.
detection wavelength of 224 nm
The CRISPR-Cas9 system was used to modify the genome.
The heatmap package in R was used to generate heatmaps and volcano plots.
The single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) mutations PHO3 $^{Asn134Asp}$ and SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ were screened, and reverse engineering verification was performed.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Excessive metabolic engineering in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.

canonical_events.summary: S. cerevisiae S26, engineered for high tyrosol, suffers from impaired growth, limiting industrial use.
ALE can restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26.
ALE protocol: automatic passaging at OD600=15 with stepwise glucose increase (20→40→80→125 g/L).
ALE performed in three stages, yielding evolved strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3 with decreasing passage times.
Fermentation in 100 g/L glucose: S26-AE2 achieved 15.33% higher biomass (17.82 g DCW/L) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol (69.20% higher than S26-AE1).
Evolved strains S26-AE2 and S26-AE3 completely consumed glucose by 12 h and produced more ethanol, indicating faster glucose utilization.
Transcriptome analysis: S26-AE2 vs S26 in 20 g/L glucose showed 36 upregulated, 1 downregulated gene; in 100 g/L, 9 upregulated, 16 downregulated.
Reduced HXK2 expression in S26-AE2 under 100 g/L glucose suggests decreased glucose repression, contributing to improved growth.
Upregulation of pyruvate synthesis genes (PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, PYK1) and TCA cycle genes in S26-AE2, indicating enhanced central metabolism.
SNP analysis of S26-AE2 identified 19 SNPs, including nonsynonymous mutations in SNZ3 (Val125Ile) and PHO3 (Asn134Asp).
Molecular docking: SNZ3 Val125Ile improved binding to D-ribose 5-phosphate (Vina score -5.7); PHO3 Asn134Asp improved binding to thiamine phosphate (Vina score -6.6).
Reverse engineering: SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation increased tyrosol by 17.01% in 100 g/L glucose, confirming its role.
Double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) increased tyrosol by only 11.37%, less than SNZ3 single mutant, suggesting possible interaction or saturation.
PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported; other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored; mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements.

edges.reason: Growth impairment problem motivates hypothesis that ALE can restore growth and improve tyrosol.
Hypothesis leads to design of ALE protocol with stepwise glucose increase.
ALE protocol executed, yielding evolved strains.
Evolved strains tested in fermentation, S26-AE2 shows best performance.
Further analysis of fermentation reveals faster glucose consumption and ethanol production.
Superior S26-AE2 selected for transcriptome analysis to understand mechanisms.
Transcriptome data reveals reduced HXK2 expression, suggesting decreased glucose repression.
Transcriptome data shows upregulation of pyruvate and TCA cycle genes.
S26-AE2 genome sequenced to identify causal mutations.
Identified mutations subjected to molecular docking to predict functional impact.
Docking results support reverse engineering validation of SNZ3 mutation.
SNZ3 mutation introduced into S26 for validation.
Double mutant tested but shows lower tyrosol increase, indicating complexity.
Partial validation and unexplored mutations highlight limitations.

must_cover_points: Initial growth impairment of S26 due to metabolic engineering.
ALE design with stepwise glucose increase and automatic passaging.
Isolation of evolved strains, especially S26-AE2 with improved growth and tyrosol.
Transcriptome analysis revealing reduced HXK2 and upregulated central metabolism.
SNP identification of SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp.
Molecular docking suggesting improved substrate binding.
Reverse engineering validation of SNZ3 mutation increasing tyrosol by 17.01%.
Double mutant underperformance and unexplored mutations as limitations.
